result:
I’ve been thinking a lot lately about the way we inherit time—how each of us is born into a topology of hours and choices carved out by strangers who never imagined us. We wake up inside their footprints and are told, brightly, that the world was built for us. Sometimes it feels more accurate to say that it was merely bequeathed, the way an atticful moth-eaten coats are bequeathed. You slip one on out of courtesy, and by noon you smell like someone else’s cedar-lined past.

Tonight the sky is doing that late-October thing where the lower edge of a cloud looks fluorescent, as if a neon bar sign were burning behind it. I walked beneath that bruised glow with a bag of groceries balanced on one hip, the plastic handles sawing into my palm, and I kept thinking: somebody taught me to call this dusk, to identify its temperature and something in the air that corresponds to memories of pumpkins and wool. The instruction came so early I can’t hear it anymore; I just obey. The entire evening turned into a demonstration of how thoroughly I have been colonized by a language I did not elect. Even the warmth of an orange streetlamp—soft as butter—carried a lecture about safety and lamplighters and the poems I was required to like in eighth grade.

What surprises me is how willingly we continue the project. I married someone, and within a year we had invented our own private parlance of references; now there are shared jokes that no one else understands and inside rituals for which we fight. That impulse to consecrate every square inch of the present with meaning feels both tender and vaguely tyrannical, like we’re trying to write graffiti on fog before somebody else’s exhalation wipes it away.

I suppose that is the work: to keep carving windows into the fog. Today I watched a toddler twist the handle of a toy steering wheel. He was parked in a plastic red coupe at the edge of a playground, engine noises sputtering out of his mouth like urgent Morse code. I caught myself envying the purity of his effort—how he wasn’t pretending to drive so much as inaugurating the very idea of motion. Somewhere in the muscle memory he will inherit from us, lanes will widen and harden, stoplights will establish their own brutal theologies, and he’ll forget that he ever planted the cosmos with the seed of a plastic horn. For now, though, steering is indistinguishable from conjuring distance the way a magician conjures coins out of your ear.

This is the kind of night that invites unsent letters. If I could write to anyone, I’d start with tomorrow’s version of myself. I’d tell her: try to remain porous enough to notice the quiet insistence of small things—like the smell of dish soap on your sleeve signaling dinner being finished, or the moment of brief silence when the dryer stops and the house exhales. Maybe chew slowly. Remember that the earlobe of the person you love is a soft miracle; touch it when they’re reading aloud and watch how words briefly tremble. And if you ever feel crowded by the ancestral hourglass you carry around, invert it—let the sand run upward just once. Watch history try to climb back to its source and marvel that it can’t.
